sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves active object right 2px within container (VERIFIED step 3)', 'LEFT': 'Moves active object left 2px within container (VERIFIED step 4)', 'UP': 'Swaps active object with nearest divider block above at same row level (VERIFIED step 5)', 'DOWN': 'Swaps active object back with divider block, reversing UP (VERIFIED step 6)', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking selects objects but only advances timer; may be needed to change which object is active (HYPOTHESIS)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'divider_blocks': 'White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, can be swapped with objects via UP/DOWN', 'colored_pixels': 'Small 2x2 movable objects inside containers on left panel', 'gray_containers': '4x8 gray boxes on left that hold colored pixels', 'white_containers': 'White frames on right panel containing colored shapes (red and blue)', 'blue_square': '2x4 blue block on left panel', 'red_blocks': 'Red and dark red blocks on left panel', 'pixel_pattern': 'Scattered blue/charcoal 1px objects forming a pattern on left'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'White horizontal bar at row 63, shrinks as timer/progress indicator', 'divider': 'Vertical line of white 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, rows 10-50'}
Current level:
- World model: Two-panel layout: left (charcoal bg) has colored objects in gray containers; right (black bg) has colored objects in white containers. Vertical white divider at col 32-33. Arrow keys move active object: LEFT/RIGHT within container, UP/DOWN swaps with divider blocks. Green pixel (node 34) is currently the active object, restored to original position after DOWN.
- Goal model: Move colored objects from left containers through divider to right side to match targets in white containers. Need to figure out how to select different objects.
- Recent findings: ['UP swaps active object with divider block above', 'DOWN reverses the swap', 'LEFT/RIGHT move within container bounds', 'Node IDs track objects across swaps - colors move with nodes']
- Open questions: ["How to select/activate different objects? (MOUSE doesn't seem to change selection)", 'What is the target configuration on the right side?', 'Does UP always go to the nearest divider block, or a specific one?', 'Can objects be moved from right side back to left?']
- Plan: Try clicking on different objects to see if selection changes, then use arrow keys to move them. Need to move all left-side objects to right-side containers.
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(20,24) on yellow pixel: only advanced timer, no gameplay change (step 1)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP didn't change the board (board_changed=False). Green stayed at [[38,12],[39,13]]. So it's stuck at the top of the container and can't go further up.
Key observation: Green's node ID changed from 34 to 33! Previously it was node 34, now it's node 33. But wait, looking at the divider blocks, node 34 is now a divider block at [[38,32],[39,33]]. So the green pixel (which was node 34) and the divider block (which was node 33) swapped IDs? No, that doesn't make sense.
Wait, let me re-read. Previously:
- Green was node 34 at [[40,12],[41,13]]
- After UP, green moved to [[38,12],[39,13]]
- Now green is node 33 at [[38,12],[39,13]]
And the divider block at [[38,32],[39,33]] is now node 34.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about what happened in the earlier UP action (step 5). When green was at [[40,10],[41,11]] and I pressed UP, it went to [[38,32],[39,33]] - the divider position. And the white divider block went to [[38,10],[39,11]]. So they swapped positions AND colors.
But now, when green was at [[40,12],[41,13]] and I pressed UP, it just moved to [[38,12],[39,13]] without swapping with the divider. It stayed green.
The key difference seems to be the column position:
- At col 10-11: UP swaps with divider (teleports to col 32-33)
- At col 12-13: UP just moves up within container
Wait, but the container boundary changed. Let me look at the container shape more carefully.
Original container (node 32) boundary: [[38, 8], [38, 13], [39, 13], [39, 12], [40, 11], [41, 11], [42, 12], [42, 13], [43, 13], [43, 8]]
After green moved to [[38,12],[39,13]], container boundary: [[38, 8], [38, 11], [39, 11], [40, 12], [40, 13], [43, 13], [43, 8]]
The container shape changed! When the green pixel is at a different position, the container boundary adjusts. The container seems to be an irregular shape that wraps around the green pixel.
Actually, I think the "container" is just the gray background area, and the green pixel moves within it. The boundary changes because the green pixel is part of the container's interior.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The green pixel is the active object. It can:
1. Move LEFT/RIGHT within the gray container
2. Move UP/DOWN within the gray container (if space exists)
3. When at the right edge (col 12-13) and pressing UP, it moves up
4. When at the left side (col 10-11) and pressing UP, it swaps with the divider
Wait, that doesn't quite make sense either. Let me think about it differently.
Maybe the container has a "door" or "exit" on the right side. When the green pixel is at the right edge of the container, it can interact with the divider. Let me try pressing RIGHT when green is at [[38,12],[39,13]] to see if it can move to the divider.
Actually, let me first try LEFT to move green back to the left side, then try UP again to see if the swap behavior is position-dependent.
Or better yet, let me try RIGHT from the current position [[38,12],[39,13]] to see if it can move to the divider at col 32-33.
Wait, I should also consider: maybe the container shape matters. The container at rows 38-39 has a narrower opening. Let me check the ascii to see the exact layout.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me move the green pixel back to its original position and then try a different sequence. Let me press LEFT to move it left, then DOWN to move it down.
Actually, I realize I should focus on understanding the container shape better. Let me look at the ascii around the container area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green at [[38,12],[39,13]], UP did nothing (at top edge)
# Node ID changed: green is now node 33, divider at row 38 is now node 34
# Let me check the ascii around the container to understand the shape

ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')

# Print rows 36-45, cols 6-40 to see container and divider area
print("Container area (rows 36-45, cols 6-40):")
for i in range(36, 46):
    if i < len(lines):
        print(f"Row {i}: {lines[i][6:40]}")

# Also check: can green move RIGHT from current position?
# Try RIGHT
action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"\nAfter RIGHT: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

UP does nothing. Container shape is irregular. Need to understand container boundaries.", "goal_model": "Move green pixel to right side? Or rearrange objects to match targets?", "recent_findings": ["UP at top edge does nothing", "Green node ID changed from 34 to 33", "Container boundary changes with green pixel position"], "open_questions": ["How to get green pixel to divider?", "What is the container shape?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Check container shape via ascii, try RIGHT from current position", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on yellow - no selection change", "UP at top edge - no change"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
